title: What Makes a TED Talk Inspiring?
text:
Group Members: Laura Henze & Tristan Zook


In this project, we used natural language processing and text mining techniques in R to investigate patterns within TED Talk transcripts. By combining transcript text with metadata such as view counts, we explored how language use may influence audience engagement.
The analysis focused on two primary questions:

Do highly viewed TED Talks use different emotional language than less popular talks?
Are certain words or themes more characteristic of popular versus unpopular TED Talks?






R
tidytext
tidyverse
ggplot2
Regular Expressions (Regex)
Sentiment Analysis
TF-IDF Analysis





The project used two datasets from the Kaggle TED Talks collection.


Included:

Speaker names
Talk titles
View counts
Tags
Publication dates
Number of comments




Included:

Full TED Talk transcripts
TED Talk URLs

The datasets were joined using TED Talk URLs, allowing transcript text to be analyzed alongside popularity metrics.





Before analysis, the transcripts were cleaned by:

Removing stop words
Removing numbers
Removing audience cues such as applause
Retaining laughter markers when relevant
Tokenizing transcript text into individual words

The cleaned dataset was then used for sentiment analysis and TF-IDF analysis.




To investigate emotional language, we applied the NRC sentiment lexicon to classify words into emotional categories.
Positive emotions included:

Joy
Trust
Anticipation
Surprise

Negative emotions included:

Fear
Anger
Sadness
Disgust

Each TED Talk received a normalized sentiment score that was compared against total view counts.






This visualization explores the relationship between emotional language and TED Talk popularity.
A slight positive relationship emerged between emotional positivity and popularity. Talks containing more optimistic and uplifting language tended to receive somewhat higher view counts on average.
However, the relationship was not especially strong. Many talks with neutral or negative sentiment still achieved high view counts, suggesting that emotional positivity alone does not determine success.
Other factors such as speaker reputation, storytelling ability, topic relevance, production quality, and timing likely play major roles in audience engagement.





Sentiment analysis captures only part of a transcript’s meaning. To investigate differences in word usage more deeply, we performed a TF-IDF analysis.
TED Talks were divided into:

Most Popular Talks (top 10% by views)
Least Popular Talks (bottom 50% by views)

TF-IDF scores were used to identify words that were particularly important within one group relative to the other.






The dumbbell plot compares the importance of words across popularity groups.
Words that were especially influential in popular talks included:

grit
kindergarten
distractions
public’s
ripples

Words that were more influential in less popular talks included:

serotonin
circuitry
intergalactic
pixels
placebo

These findings suggest that highly viewed TED Talks may rely more heavily on broadly relatable language, while less popular talks often contain more technical or specialized vocabulary.
While these conclusions are somewhat speculative, they provide insight into how communication style may influence audience engagement.







Positive sentiment showed a modest association with TED Talk popularity.



Highly viewed TED Talks tended to emphasize language that was accessible to a broader audience.



Popularity is likely influenced by many factors beyond language alone, including:

Speaker reputation
Storytelling ability
Topic relevance
Timing
Production quality






Through this project, I gained experience with:

Natural Language Processing (NLP)
Text Mining
Sentiment Analysis
TF-IDF Analysis
Regular Expressions
Data Cleaning
Data Visualization
Scientific Communication





This project demonstrated how computational text analysis can uncover patterns that may not be obvious through casual observation alone. By combining transcript text with audience engagement metrics, we were able to investigate how language, emotion, and communication style interact within one of the world’s most influential public speaking platforms.
The project strengthened my skills in data wrangling, visualization, natural language processing, and communicating technical findings to a broader audience.




View Full Project Report
View GitHub Repository




TED Talks Dataset (Kaggle)

title: Additional Project
text:
This project was completed as part of my Statistics for Data Science coursework and explored factors associated with individual medical insurance charges. Using a publicly available insurance dataset, I investigated how demographic and health-related variables influence healthcare costs and applied statistical and data visualization techniques to identify important patterns.



The primary goal of the project was to determine which factors were most strongly associated with insurance charges and to evaluate the relationships among variables such as age, body mass index (BMI), smoking status, number of children, sex, and geographic region.



The analysis was conducted in R using data visualization and statistical modeling techniques. Exploratory data analysis was performed using ggplot2 to identify trends and potential relationships within the data. Variables were compared through summary statistics and visualizations, and regression methods were used to quantify the effects of different predictors on insurance charges.




Smoking status was the strongest predictor of increased insurance charges.
Age was positively associated with healthcare costs, with older individuals generally experiencing higher charges.
BMI also showed a positive relationship with charges, particularly among smokers.
Sex and geographic region had comparatively smaller effects on overall insurance costs.
Visualizations helped highlight interactions among variables and identify trends that were not immediately apparent from summary statistics alone.





R Programming
Data Cleaning and Exploration
Data Visualization with ggplot2
Statistical Modeling
Regression Analysis
Interpretation and Communication of Results




This project strengthened my ability to work with real-world datasets and communicate statistical findings through visualizations and written analysis. It also reinforced the importance of exploratory data analysis in understanding complex relationships within data before applying formal statistical models. Through this project, I gained additional experience using R as a tool for reproducible data analysis and scientific communication.

title: Home
text:
Hi there! I’m Laura (pronounced “LOR-uh”). I am an Undergraduate Student at Lawrence University studying Biochemistry, Data Science, and Anthropology. I’m a Wisconsin native and have kept my roots here ever since I was born. Prior to my time at Lawrence, I graduated near the top of my class from my alma mater, Plymouth High School, where I first developed my love for science.
In the future, I plan to attend a Forensic Science graduate institute in order to specialize in Forensic Toxicology. My academic interests combine laboratory science, analytical chemistry, and computational analysis.
One of my favorite courses at Lawrence has been analytical chemistry, where I developed strong laboratory skills and gained experience with precise quantitative techniques and scientific methodology. I especially enjoyed spending long hours in the lab focusing on accuracy, troubleshooting, and reproducible scientific work.
Through my explorations of research at Lawrence, I came to discover that data processing was something I hadn’t previously considered, but quickly realized was an incredibly valuable skill to have. With this, I discovered my enjoyment of data science, creating informative figures, and using computational analysis to support technical lab reports and scientific communication.



Biochemistry
Analytical Chemistry
Forensic Toxicology
Scientific Research
Data Science
Data Visualization

In addition to my academics, I compete on the Lawrence Cross Country and Track and Field teams in my extra time. Running has continued to be one of my passions since I was young, and I’m very grateful to represent my team here.
In addition to running and my studies, I also enjoy singing, watching movies, playing videogames, and practicing my flute (when I have the time).

title: pristineR Package
text:
pristineR is an R package designed to simplify common data-cleaning and summarization tasks through safer, user-friendly helper functions.
The package focuses on preprocessing issues commonly encountered in real-world datasets, including:

inconsistent date formats
messy column names
and missing values

This project was developed as part of my data science coursework and helped strengthen my experience with package development, documentation, reproducible workflows, and GitHub collaboration.

title: pristineR Package
text:
pristineR is an R package designed to simplify common data-cleaning and summarization tasks through safer, user-friendly helper functions.
The package focuses on preprocessing issues commonly encountered in real-world datasets, including:

inconsistent date formats
messy column names
and missing values

This project was developed as part of my data science coursework and helped strengthen my experience with package development, documentation, reproducible workflows, and GitHub collaboration.

title: pristineR Package
text:
Motivation
Many datasets require extensive cleaning before analysis can begin. I created pristineR to streamline repetitive preprocessing tasks into lightweight helper functions that are easy to use and designed to fail safely when invalid inputs are encountered.
This project also gave me experience thinking about usability and documentation from the perspective of future users.

title: pristineR Package
text:
Why pristineR?
Many real-world datasets contain inconsistent date formats, messy column names, and missing values that require repetitive cleaning before analysis can begin.
pristineR was created to simplify these common preprocessing tasks through lightweight helper functions that are easy to use, well documented, and designed to fail safely when given invalid inputs.

title: pristineR Package
text:
Installation
You can install the development version of pristineR from GitHub with:
# install.packages("remotes")
# install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("ADC-405-S26/pristineR")

title: pristineR Package
text:
Load the Package
library(pristineR)

title: pristineR Package
text:
Example Dataset
The package contains an example dataset called messy_survey.
The dataset intentionally contains inconsistent column names, mixed date formats, and missing values.

title: pristineR Package
text:
Clean Column Names
Datasets imported from spreadsheets or surveys often contain spaces, punctuation, or inconsistent capitalization in column names.
The clean_names() function converts messy column names into snake_case format.
names(messy_survey)

cleaned_data <- clean_names(messy_survey)

names(cleaned_data)

title: pristineR Package
text:
Parse Dates
Datasets frequently contain dates stored in multiple formats.
The safe_date_parse() function safely converts these values into standard Date objects.
cleaned_data$submission_date <-
  safe_date_parse(cleaned_data$submission_date)

cleaned_data

title: pristineR Package
text:
Handling Invalid Dates
If a date cannot be parsed, safe_date_parse() returns NA and produces a warning message instead of stopping execution completely.
safe_date_parse(c("2024-01-01", "bad-date"))

title: pristineR Package
text:
Generate Summary Statistics
The quick_summary() function generates common summary statistics for selected numeric columns.
quick_summary(
  cleaned_data,
  c("exam_score")
)
The summary includes:

mean
standard deviation
minimum
maximum
missing value counts

title: pristineR Package
text:
Function Parameters

safe_date_parse(x)

x: a character vector containing date values



quick_summary(data, columns)

data: a data frame
columns: character vector of numeric columns to summarize



clean_names(data)

data: a data frame with column names to clean

title: pristineR Package
text:
Additional Example
pristineR can also be used with other messy datasets.
example_data <- data.frame(
  "Customer Name" = c("Alice", "Bob"),
  "Purchase.Date" = c("2024-01-01", "01/15/2024"),
  "Total Sales $" = c(120, 95)
)

example_data <- clean_names(example_data)

example_data$purchase_date <-
  safe_date_parse(example_data$purchase_date)

example_data

title: pristineR Package
text:
Package Functions
pristineR currently includes:

safe_date_parse()
quick_summary()
clean_names()

title: pristineR Package
text:
Skills Developed
Through this project, I gained experience with:

R package development
Documentation writing
GitHub workflows
Reproducible analysis
Data cleaning pipelines
Error handling
User-centered function design

title: Analyzing Himalayan Climbing Expeditions with SQL and R
text:
Group Members: Laura Henze and Tristan Zook


In this project, we used SQL and R to analyze historical Himalayan climbing expedition data from the Himalayan Expedition Dataset. Our goal was to investigate patterns in expedition success, climber safety, participation, and staffing.
The project involved:

Building a relational database
Importing and cleaning data
Writing SQL queries
Visualizing results in R
Interpreting expedition trends




We worked with three datasets:

members
expeditions
peaks

These datasets were imported into a DuckDB database and linked using expedition and peak identifiers.



The database consisted of three tables:


Included information such as:

Age
Citizenship
Expedition role
Success status
Oxygen use
Injury status




Included:

Year
Season
Peak
Number of members
Number of hired staff
Expedition outcome




Included:

Peak name
Height
Climbing status
First ascent information





One question we investigated was whether climbers using supplemental oxygen were more likely to successfully summit.
SELECT
  m.oxygen_used,
  COUNT(*) AS total_climbers,
  SUM(CASE WHEN m.success = TRUE THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS successes,
  ROUND(
    SUM(CASE WHEN m.success = TRUE THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) * 1.0 /
    COUNT(*),
    3
  ) AS success_rate
FROM members m
GROUP BY m.oxygen_used;
This query compared summit success rates between climbers who used supplemental oxygen and those who did not.





We observed a general increase in expedition success rates over the years, likely due to improvements in equipment, training, and expedition planning.



Climbers using supplemental oxygen had noticeably higher success rates.



A visualization of annual death rates showed a long-term decrease in climber mortality despite growing expedition participation.



Nepal contributed the largest number of climbers, likely due to both geography and the important role Nepali guides play in Himalayan expeditions.









SQL
Database Design
Data Cleaning
Data Visualization
DuckDB
R Programming
Relational Databases
Scientific Communication




This project helped me develop experience building and querying relational databases while connecting SQL analysis to visualization and interpretation in R. It strengthened my understanding of the complete data analysis workflow from raw data to meaningful conclusions.



View Full Project Report
View GitHub Repository
